speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Henry Wiener. The date is February 4, 1996. You were telling me about your experiences with your uncle. What town was this again? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stary Wisnicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stary Wisnicz, right. But I've remembered to ask you, were you-- where were you when the Germans first came in? You were still in Chorzow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just boarding the-- the-- the train with my father. It was the first day, it was Friday, the September 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were actually en route to Stary Wisnicz when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were en route, but where, we didn't know. Just out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, did you see anything? Did you see the Germans come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So how long did you stay with your uncle in Stary Wisnicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right when they started to give us trouble, we-- we found a place in Wisnicz Nowy. We moved there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1940-- end of 1940. Something of the sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed with the uncle nearly a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go in Wisnicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to-- we rent a sort of place they used to have-- an oil depot room. So we-- we made this our-- our home, sort of. My mother decorated and put the separation for us and for the children. And, you know, one of those things was ours. You know, it was cold in winter, very cold, because it was a cement floor. You know, and it was not comfortable. But we were family. We were together, that's what counts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had your sister been doing? She was still quite young then. Was she going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At your uncle's?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no school for Jewish children. But there were some separate things that we were teaching her. And some other people were teaching her. The school, per se, was no school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did-- how did your sister, and your mother, and your brother-- how did they occupy themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we were working, as I told you. We were cutting wood. There we were renting ourselves out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my sister or my mother, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, in the new place in Wisnicz Nowy, what happened there? How did you-- how did you live there? We lived there until 1942. In the meantime, I met Sally, so-- Sally is your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about when you settled into Wisnicz Nowy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- I wouldn't use the words part of a community, but we stood there, and we tried to make the best of it. But we never lost hope that the things would come to an end and we'd persevere. And that's what kept not only us-- I think everybody had the same-- not opinion, but hope-- that this would have to come to an end, sooner or later. And we did not know that it was only the beginning of the end. Because at that time, compared to others, it wasn't that very bad-- comparing to ghettos. Because this was still open areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there-- were there rules being made then, or still the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always the same rules. Always, it was tight. Eventually, it became a rule in 1942, that all Jews must evacuate Wisnicz Nowy, and surroundings, and they would be all sent to labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that, tell me a little bit more. You said you met Sally at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I met Sally.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told you probably that whatever this-- I worked with-- with some girls from there, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in the castle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which castle was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a castle in Wisnicz Nowy-- it was a castle from the time of King Sobieski in Poland who was instrumental in stopping Genghis Khan in Vienna. Someplace there. So it must have been 400, 500 years old. And the Germans had a headquarters there. There was a POW camp of Germans over there. So they had X amount of Jews to do manual labor. So I was one of the manual laborers, and the girls were also part of it. That's how we got together. They said they had some friends to meet, and we met separately. We tried to make as much social life as possible, so we shouldn't be stagnant, so to speak. So that's how everything started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of social activities took place at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no social activities. We were not allowed to any meetings at all. Everything was on penalty of death. No meeting, no nothing. But we got together anyway. You know, my brother got together with his age group. You know, we young-- one year makes a lot of difference. So he got together with his age group, I got together with my age group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the houses. Because people had their own houses. And we had-- some-- some nights we went-- as a matter of fact, sometimes we were dancing also, if you call it this way. Someone's harmonica, or something. We shouldn't-- we shouldn't be all black. We should always have hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get news from the war? Was there a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only hearsay. Because you couldn't have a radio. No way-- now way could you have a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if you had a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Death. Everything is death. You're here to a foreign station. That was your death. There was no-- there was no other laws. Everything was penalty of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody said he heard something from some Polish who heard underground this. And you believe it or not. I mean, everything was hearsay.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you read the German papers, you could read between lines. If you get a hold of a paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, they-- like in Stalingrad, they were saying that they-- they're withdrawing according to the plan. So according to the plan, you knew they were in trouble. Every time they were in trouble, there were more Jews being killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anywhere. Anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt that if the Germans were not doing well, they would take it out on the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They'll take it out any way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did people disappear from your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at that time. But they-- when they-- when we heard that the area had to be liquidated, that-- according to this, and people sent to camps, that time we went to-- we decided not to go. Not to listen to this. And we made an arrangement-- arrangement with a Polish farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: In June of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The summer of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the order was given to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The order was given that all the Jews from surrounding areas had to clear the area, period.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said in the labor camps. Different area.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they clear-- did they ask you to come together to be transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, depot. Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family says, no way. And I went to Sally's family, and said, listen, there's no such thing at the depot. So they went-- Sally want that I should come with them, and I wanted Sally to come with us. But we decided to go with our families.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did your family go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a farmer. And we stayed there for about a week or so. Four of us. Because my brother worked for the Germans. They were building truck frames-- frames for trucks. So he was sort of-- he had a pass, so he was free of all this. And he says, go ahead. He was the one who told us, go ahead, hide. When it will be all clear, I'll come after you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all clear, and everybody was coming-- the Bochnia ghetto was nearby. So when they came to-- you know-- when it was all clear, and everybody was sort of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Jew-free area-- free of Jews area-- in Sally's town, and surroundings. There were no more Jews left. So after that, my brother said, there's no sense staying there. Come to Bochnia ghetto. Where was your brother? Where was he staying while he was working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was-- he was staying there, on-- on the-- on the-- on the premises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you had lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not where we had lived. He worked in the Bochnia ghetto. So he was travelling there, to Bochnia ghetto. So he was-- because he happened to have a pass, because he was a mechanic and he was mechanically inclined. So he was working there. And so when this happened, we went to Bochnia ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the farmer where you hid? You remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to connect to this farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. My father connected with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents-- did your family pay him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Whatever we could, we paid him. Some clothing, something, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't guide me, we were staying by him.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you went to the farmer, were you hidden during the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the cellar, all this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were searches being made for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that-- not that far. Only in the houses, where they used to live. That's where the searches were made, but not outside the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I mentioned my brother gave us a message we should come. And also, you had to smuggle yourself in. You just couldn't go this way. How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night-- during the night, we went. Then he told us more or less which way to go. We went. It wasn't so strict at that time like later on. And we stayed there for a while, in ghetto, itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in the ghetto did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. This area designated for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a room. We-- my-- because a lot of people were evacuated from the area. Then they made the ghetto. So there was a room at that time. We came with my aunt, also. We gave her what used to be, in the beginning-- we had to come together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which aunt was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Malka. She was my father's younger sister. We were very close together with her. Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Bochnia ghetto for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bochnia ghetto-- [SIGHS] everybody had a room, only the family had the room. And some of them lived there before. They were not refugees, or they came from there. They were moved from one place to another. And they were divided into areas-- one, a working area. One, a non-working area. And the Germans saw to it that not everybody is working. So they had to-- ones who are they make-- they made-- they killed people, period. They killed-- the area with-- people who did not work, could not produce passes that they're working. They killed a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the open street?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the open street. Go to houses, kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were hidden that time. We were [NON-ENGLISH], we were [NON-ENGLISH]. My brother told us one time, he says, look-- it was in March in '43-- he says, look, they're supposed to liquidate the area B ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made bunkers for ourselves. He covered everything. Someone had to be outside covering everything. And we were hidden. After we heard the shots, silence, everything, we came out. You were in this area B?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not working in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you couldn't get a pass. They gave passes mostly to people who come from there. You know, you had to know somebody-- who is who. So we were refugees. You know-- one of those things. Everybody says the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to live in the ghetto? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some houses were deteriorated from this, so we bought the remnants of the house. We cut up the wood. And we sold the wood to people for the wintertime. You know-- we persevered. For bread, we had enough for bread. In the meantime, I worked outside. I started-- I got-- No, I was caught at the time, and sent to a labor camp in Plaszow. Labor, not concentration camp. It was a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 19-- beginning of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you caught? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say you're walking in the street. They catch you. It's the law. We need so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken off the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken off the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what day it was? Was it in the middle of the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, middle of the week. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me to Plaszow. I was there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- you stayed in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I stayed in Plaszow. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know where you had gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I-- I gave them a message. I sent them-- it wasn't that far from Bochnia to Krakow. It wasn't that far. So whoever was there, I gave them my message. In the message I said, I'm all right. You know--young guy, 18 years old. You know-- if you don't have high hopes, you're just-- you're down to earth, and you more or less absorb it, so to speak, quote unquote. And I spoke German also, so I acquainted with all the German soldiers who were issuing passes. So he gave me a pass and he told me I should issue passes. And he signed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the passes for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passes that you could go home and come back. There were passes for certain [INAUDIBLE]. So every Friday night, really, I tried to go home to Bochnia. And they didn't touch me, because I had a pass that I am working. So they didn't touch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working there by earth moving. Simmons Bauer is still now-- the Germans still have it. It's a very big company. And we were laying tracks. And I was part of a group like this. And once in a while, I was going-- going sort of home to Bochnia ghetto. And it was there. And this happened to me two or three times. By the fourth time-- they were catching me-- by the fourth time, they caught my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father was in-- in the 40s, at that time. So I went ahead and I said, I'll be smart. I said look, I exchange for my father. OK? What do you care? You need so many people. What do you mean that they caught?

speaker: interviewer
text: Off the street. They caught you off the street in the-- in the ghetto B, the people who are not working to-- to camp. And I saw the same thing happened again. So I went and I exchanged for my father. I said, Dad. He says, you go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what are they going to do? I have a pass. I don't want to show the pass here, but I will show the pass over there that I don't belong to them. They caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the third time-- but at the time, they caught me and they sent me to a concentration camp, in-- in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1943 in-- it was in the end of summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first year you did in a working part of Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, working part of Plaszow. This is a concentration camp. There were two different areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the working part of Plaszow. Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge of you? Who supervised, who guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. But there was Jewish, and then Germans. But there was no hitting or anything in the labor camps. There was no hitting. Here, you know, the German masters, they were hitting-- if they wanted to. They had full power of life and death, if they wanted to. But I spoke German, so I didn't-- more or less, you had to play by ear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most people in the working camp able to survive that experience? Did you see most of them just making it through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them, yeah, at that time. At that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there adequate food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was never adequate food. But you managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they give you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave you soup and a piece of bread. It-- it was-- was sort of not enough to live, and too much to die-- type of thing-- in the labor camp. In the concentration camp, it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody else in the working camp? Did you know any of the people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some. Some. But nobody close, really, I could consider friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going through this alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. More or less, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your family managing during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was working, as I told you, for the Germans. And my father and my uncle, my aunt's husband, they were both doing these things with the wood. And they were managing in the ghetto. But there always a constant fear of-- of killing, because they were like an open season over there, in the ghetto B.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people disappearing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not disappearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, did you know people were being killed? Did you see this one was here today and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They would kill people on the outside of the ghetto that were caught without passes, without-- they say, oh, they caught some Jewish girls who somebody pointed at them, that they were Jewish-- Jewesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But you had heard about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you were sent to this concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was sent to Plaszow, things were entirely different. First of all, you were surrounded by barbed wire, all around you. You couldn't get out of there. You-- you were part of a barrack. And the barrack was called Bochnia Barrack, because all those who was caught there-- there was from Bochnia ghetto. And-- And you were under strict orders, working area, where you got up 6 o'clock in the morning and you worked until dusk. And-- and the kapo-- there was a Jewish kapo-- who-- or a Jewish supervisor-- who beat you quite a while. It did not happen to me, but I saw it happening. And a German master was over all you, and he was-- he was a master of life and death, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on top of it, the commander of the camp was the famous Goeth. And he was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how do you describe him? Human hell. Because no matter where you worked, you worked. And he was always going to the gun, and-- and-- and the German shepherd with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you-- if you looked at him, he didn't let you look. He just shot you, that's all. He would let the dog run over you, without provocation, without anything, without ifs or buts. In the meantime, from our barracks, somebody ran away. And they took 10 people from the barracks at random, in the middle of the camp, and they shot them-- over the four who were running away. The second time someone ran away, again, and they shot 20. At random.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- in the meantime, I was with a friend of mine, also from Wisnicz. His name was Abush Reich, was his name. I said, Abush, the law of averages are against us. I said, we have to get out of here. We were walking one Sunday afternoon-- we had the free time only Sunday afternoon. We were walking and we saw a big line over there. So we asked-- in the camp itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I heard the whole Bochnia ghetto is liquidated. And there's nobody left there-- in ghetto B. In the total ghetto, the total ghetto B is being liquidated. So nobody told me something about what happened to my family. Nothing. Prior to that, I forgot to tell you, my brother was arrested by the Jewish police in Bochnia ghetto, and he was accused of belonging to the Jewish underground. And that's the last I heard of him. I had in my mind to tell you this, but something happened. And that's the last I heard of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. And I didn't know, because he didn't want to tell me. Because if he tell me, and they catch me, I would sort of spill the beans. I just found out from somebody-- somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew that he was a [NON-ENGLISH], like an interrogation center in Krakow, and then he was sent to Auschwitz. So in the meantime, I knew that when they liquidated the ghetto that nobody was surviving. So I said to Abush, I said, Abush, we have to get out of here. This doesn't-- doesn't work. So there was a big line over there. I said, what's this line for? They said, you know what? This line is for a guy named Schindler-- Oskar Schindler. I said, who is he? He said, he needs some people to work for him outside the ghetto. I said, Abush, I don't care where he is. As long as we get out of here, we stay in line. We stayed in line, standing with about 400 people. How many people he needs? He said, about 100. I said, Abush, we're never going to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, the Jewish police, the camp Jewish police are minding order in line. And they're hitting here, hitting there. One guy called somebody. He said, Steiner, just take care of this over here. I heard the word, Steiner. And my mother's name older is Moehler, at that time, many names they have. And she came from a Steiner family. So I-- I go up the line, and the guy wanted to hit me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, wait, Steiner. I said, hold it, hold-- I said, I think that we are related. We are related? You and I? I says, maybe. I said, do you come from Bielsko? He said, yeah, what's your business? I says, I think that my-- you and my mother are cousins. He said, where do you come-- what's your mother's name? I said, Sara, [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, [NON-ENGLISH], where do you come from? I said, Chorzow. Are you Sarah's son? I says, yeah. What are you doing here? What do you mean, what am I doing here? Look at me, I'm not waiting for a bus. Of course, I'm doing here-- I want to go to-- out of here. I said, I'm from Bochnia Barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK. So he said, come with me. I said, look, I have a friend of mine and we're going together. He grabbed us both, put in front of the line. This how I end up with Schindler. So you never can tell, you see, how things turn out. That's how I ended up by Schindler, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then, after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was by Schindler, I was working--

speaker: interviewer
text: That day. No, what happened that day? So you got-- you were taken to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken to Schindler. Right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened that day, when you were taken to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assigned to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To outside. Schindler has his barracks outside the camp, in Amalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had separate barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separate barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were staying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were staying there. We were staying there, we' had a sign over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what that was? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Must have been the end of summer '43, or beginning of fall '43. I don't remember the exact date. I knew only it was a good day at that time. We were assigned to a special group of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] construction. We had the foreman, and we had thin boys like me working construction outside. Not inside. Inside was nice and warm. But I said, I don't care. Whatever it is, it's better than over there. That's how we wound up by Schindler. And we worked there. And anything there, compared to Plaszow, was-- was heaven, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What happened is this. At one time, Oskar Schindler always was coming. He said, how are you? How are you doing? You know, plain words. So we told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one Sunday afternoon, he gets the guy, the chief of the Jewish police, he said, let them play a little bit of ball in the afternoon. They gave us a soccer ball and we played in the afternoon. So I-- I was good player, so I scored a couple of goals that time. So the chief of police says, why-- where-- who are you? Where do you work? He came to me, says, oh, I see you're a good player. Where do you work? I said, in construction. He said, why do you work construction? He said, come on, I'll give you a job in inside. And I said, look, I have a policy. sort of a policy-- we work together. I don't push, I don't press. I want to work with him together. He said, any time you want to come inside, tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks later, the whole police, the whole higher echelon was send to Mauthausen. Everyone except the construction, except us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish, all Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Part of Schindler's group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of Schindler's group. They all were sent to Mauthausen. All. This group remained, and a few more.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the inside of the camp, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: From inside the camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler's whole camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler's camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Goeth must have, or somebody else must have told him we have too many workers here-- complained, for the production that you're issuing, you have to liquidate. So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he get those workers back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, once they went to Mauthausen, they were gone. They were gone there. Most of them, a lot of them persevered. But you know, we stayed there. And-- and eventually, something happened. I'll tell you later. OK. We'll pause here for a tape change.
